name: msf-phase3-outline
label: MSF Phase 3 — Outline
description: Compact near-future science-fiction (MSF) outline development. Ported from the n8n "MSF Phase 3 — Outline" workflow (Mercury). Takes the Phase-2 developmental artifacts (concept, world guide, characters) plus the book's MSF genre guide and builds a disciplined chapter-by-chapter outline, then runs the source workflow's quality chain on it: flesh out the outline -> MSF structural-compliance review -> apply fixes -> chapter-engagement review -> apply fixes -> generate chapter titles -> reconcile the character profiles against the final outline -> produce a scene-details reference for the prose writer. The result is the {{chapterCount}}-chapter outline (~{{wordsPerChapter}} words/chapter) plus reconciled character profiles and a scene-details document the production pipeline consumes. Linear chain (no per-chapter loop). Per-step models match the n8n source via OpenRouter and are provisional: Flesh Out on claude-opus-4-7; the MSF/engagement/title passes on gemini-3.1-pro-preview; character reconciliation + scene details on claude-sonnet-4-6.

steps.label: Flesh Out Book Outline
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a structural editor specialising in compact near-future science fiction. Your job is to build a chapter-by-chapter outline for a novel that does its work efficiently, no filler, no bloat, no chapters that exist only to fill space. You are not writing prose. You are designing a machine that, when expanded into prose, will produce a focused, disciplined MSF novel at the target length.

Work from the developmental artifacts (concept, world guide, characters) in your context above and the book's MSF genre guide in your context (its tropes and required elements). The book is "{{title}}".

OBJECTIVE: Produce a {{chapterCount}}-chapter outline that hits the book's target length when expanded to prose, averaging about {{wordsPerChapter}} words per chapter. Every chapter must earn its existence. Every scene must do three jobs simultaneously. Every ending must leave something unresolved. The outline is the architecture; the prose writers who follow you will fill it in. Your job is to make sure the structure holds weight.

TARGET SPECIFICATION:
- Chapter count: {{chapterCount}} chapters.
- Chapter average: about {{wordsPerChapter}} words per chapter.
- Chapter minimum: short punchy chapters (aftermath, reversal, decision) may run well under the average.
- Chapter maximum: key structural chapters (commitment, crisis, resolution) may run well over the average.

COMPACT DISCIPLINE. This is a compact novel. Compact does not mean thin, it means that nothing is present that does not need to be present. Apply these tests before finalising each chapter:
- TEST 1 REMOVAL TEST: What would be lost if this chapter were cut entirely? If the answer can be summarized in two sentences ("the reader learns X and Y"), the chapter is not carrying enough weight. Either give it a third job or fold it.
- TEST 2 TRIPLE DUTY TEST: Does this chapter simultaneously advance the plot, develop the protagonist's arc, AND reveal something about the world's institutional reality? If it only does two, find the third or restructure.
- TEST 3 EXPERTISE TEST: Is the protagonist's specific professional knowledge load-bearing in this chapter? If the scene could happen to any character in any profession, it belongs off-page or needs to be reframed around the protagonist's expertise.
- TEST 4 CHAPTER-END TENSION TEST: Does the chapter end with an open question, a wrong conclusion the protagonist has just reached, or a decision whose consequences are not yet visible? If the protagonist is at rest at the chapter's end (except at the structural midpoint false resolution), restructure the ending.

STRUCTURAL FRAMEWORK. Build across four movements, scaled to {{chapterCount}} chapters:
- ACT 1 "The Cage Closes" (first ~25%): Establish the protagonist in their professional world before the inciting event. Show what they have, the expertise, the position, the thing they will lose. The inciting event must intrude on the protagonist's professional world, not just their personal life. By the end of Act 1 the protagonist has made the first irreversible decision (Commitment, ~30%). They cannot go back. The cage has closed around them. Beats: protagonist at work establishing expertise and institutional position and what they value; inciting event intrudes and the professional world is disrupted; first attempt to resolve within the rules fails and the failure costs something; Discovery (~20%) reframes the situation; Commitment (~30%) the irreversible decision.
- ACT 2A "The Cost Accumulates" (next ~30%): The protagonist pursues their committed course. Initial apparent progress gives way to the first real costs. By the midpoint something the protagonist valued is permanently gone. The false resolution at the midpoint should feel like a genuine partial victory, and be wrong. Beats: protagonist moves forward with professional competence in action and early apparent progress; first real cost, something lost that cannot be recovered, the antagonist force responds; Complication (~55%) the cost of the Commitment becomes fully visible; Midpoint brief false resolution.
- ACT 2B "The Cage Tightens" (next ~25%): Each move the protagonist makes closes off another option. The antagonist force does not need to be actively malicious, it simply continues its rational institutional operations, and those operations are now incompatible with what the protagonist needs. Beats: escalating institutional pressure, options close one by one; false bottom, protagonist believes they have found a solution; Crisis (~80%) the false bottom collapses and the protagonist is stripped of remaining options.
- ACT 3 "Resolution and Consequence" (final ~20%): The protagonist at their lowest point, then the irreversible decision (~95%), then consequence. The ending must show what the decision cost, not celebrate what it achieved. Ambiguous or conditional resolution is preferred over clean triumph. The final chapter should leave the reader with a question, not an answer. Beats: protagonist stripped and at lowest point forced to see clearly; the climactic decision that cannot be undone (~95%); consequence, cost shown not triumph celebrated, world continues, question left open.

Map each turning point to a specific chapter and approximate position: Discovery ~20%, Commitment ~30%, Complication ~55%, Crisis ~80%, Climactic decision ~95%.

MSF OUTLINE DISCIPLINES:
- MORAL RATCHET: Map the protagonist's moral compromise arc across all chapters. Each compromise must follow logically from the last. The reader should be able to trace the line from the protagonist's first small transgression in Act 1 to the large irreversible act near the climax. If any step in the chain requires a leap of logic, add an intermediate step or strengthen the causal link. For each chapter, state where the protagonist stands on the moral ratchet.
- INFORMATION TENSION: Track what the reader knows versus what the protagonist knows at each chapter boundary. MSF runs on asymmetry: reader slightly ahead (dread) or reader slightly behind (anticipation). Never exactly even. Flag chapters where the asymmetry is absent.
- ANTAGONIST PRESENCE: The institutional opposition must be present in every chapter, not necessarily as an active threat, but as a weight the protagonist is working against or around. Even chapters where the antagonist force does not appear directly should show its shadow: a regulation the protagonist must route around, a colleague who has chosen compliance, a system working exactly as designed and against the protagonist anyway.
- EXPERTISE IN ACTION: In the large majority of chapters, the protagonist's specific professional knowledge must determine the outcome of a scene, a decision only they could make, a detail only they would notice, an interpretation only their training produces. Flag any chapter where the expertise is decorative rather than load-bearing.
- SECONDARY CHARACTER ARCS: Each secondary character who appears in 2+ chapters must have a tracked trajectory. They are not props for the protagonist's arc, they are institutional actors with their own pressures, evolving in response to the same events. Map each secondary character's position across the chapters they appear in: where they start, what shifts their stance, where they end. A secondary character who appears in several chapters with the same stance is either redundant or wasted.
- THEMATIC PRESSURE: The novel's thematic question must be pressed across the chapters in multiple explicit moments (at least roughly a third of the chapters), with competing institutional positions offering different answers. A novel that names a thematic question but only engages it once does not have a working theme, it has a tagline. The thematic question should be pressed by different institutional voices across the arc, with the climactic decision responding to it directly.

TASK:
1. Extract the plot structure from the concept, inciting event, all turning points, the protagonist's key decisions, and the ending tendency. These are your structural anchors. Do not deviate from the concept's established turning points; the outline maps them into chapters.
2. Map the character arcs from the character profiles onto the chapter structure. Know, for each chapter, where the protagonist is on their arc, what certainty they still hold, what they have already compromised, what is at stake personally.
3. Build the chapters. For each, run all four compact discipline tests before finalising. Flag any chapter that fails a test rather than forcing it through. It is better to flag a structural problem than to paper over it with a weak chapter.
4. Produce the structural overview: turning point map, moral ratchet map, information asymmetry schedule, word count by act.
5. Check: does the outline honour the required elements from the genre guide? Does at least one required element play a structural role rather than appearing as background detail?

OUTPUT. Produce the complete outline. Begin with a STRUCTURAL OVERVIEW: working title (can differ from the concept title if stronger); a one-sentence logline (WHO + WHAT TECHNOLOGY/SITUATION + WHAT IS AT STAKE); a word-count plan by act; a turning-point map (each of the five turning points with its chapter and approximate position and a one-sentence summary); a moral-ratchet map (each chapter where the protagonist makes a moral compromise, in order, with what they compromise and what it follows from); an information-asymmetry schedule (the key reveals, what the reader learns vs. what the protagonist still does not know); a secondary-character-arc map (for each secondary character in 2+ chapters: starting position, key shifts tied to chapters, ending position); a thematic-pressure schedule (which chapters press the thematic question, which institutional position presses it, and the competing answers); and a required-element-integration note (which genre-guide required element is active and in which chapters it plays a structural role).

Then, for EVERY chapter, give: number; a specific (not generic) title that creates pull on its own; word target; act; a TRIPLE-DUTY breakdown (one sentence each for plot / character / world); a 300-400 word summary that names the scenes, decisions, and exchanges, with the protagonist's expertise visible and the antagonist force's presence (direct or indirect) visible, ending on the chapter-end tension not on resolution; an OPENING HOOK (the specific beat that opens the chapter, in media res where possible, with tension already present); a CHAPTER-END TENSION (the specific unresolved question or destabilised state, a live wire not a cliffhanger); a MORAL-RATCHET STATE (where the protagonist stands on the compromise arc at chapter's end); SECONDARY-CHARACTER STATES (for each secondary character present: name, current stance, the institutional pressure they navigate, what this chapter contributes to their arc, or "None present"); a THEMATIC-PRESSURE note ("Not engaged" or which institutional position presses the question and the answer/counter-answer this chapter contributes); a primary ENGAGEMENT DIMENSION (one of: decision_momentum, information_tension, moral_ratchet, systemic_revelation, personal_cost_escalation, chapter_pull); and DISCIPLINE FLAGS (PASS, or any of the four compact discipline tests this chapter does not fully satisfy with a note on what would resolve it).

Finally add a STRUCTURAL FLAGS section listing any structural problems the author should resolve before prose expansion, or "None, outline is structurally sound." Every chapter must be present and complete, do not truncate later chapters. This is a production artifact, output the outline only.

steps.label: MSF Outline Review
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a structural editor and MSF compliance auditor. You have just received the complete {{chapterCount}}-chapter novel outline for "{{title}}" produced in the step above. Your job is to audit it against MSF structural standards and identify every structural problem that, if left unfixed, will produce a flawed novel regardless of how well the prose is written.

You are not reviewing prose. You are reviewing architecture. A beautiful sentence cannot fix a chapter that fails the Triple Duty test. A compelling voice cannot rescue a moral ratchet with a broken link. Find the structural failures before prose expansion begins.

OBJECTIVE: Produce a prioritised list of every structural fix the outline requires. Include every CRITICAL and HIGH severity problem. Include MEDIUM severity problems where the fix is surgical (reframing, repositioning, sharpening) and does not require inventing new plot. Skip LOW severity polish for this pass. Do not impose an artificial cap, a clean outline may have 2 fixes; a problematic outline may have 12. Rank by impact, not by chapter order. Be specific about which chapter, which element, and what the fix requires.

MSF STRUCTURAL STANDARDS:
1. COMPACT DISCIPLINE. Every chapter must pass all four tests. REMOVAL: if cut, would the loss be more than two sentences of exposition? If not, the chapter is not load-bearing. TRIPLE DUTY: does the chapter simultaneously advance the external plot, develop the protagonist's arc, AND reveal something about institutional/systemic reality? Two of three is a failure. EXPERTISE: is the protagonist's specific professional knowledge load-bearing, a decision only they could make, a detail only they would notice? If any professional could play this scene, it fails. CHAPTER-END TENSION: does the chapter end on an open question, a wrong conclusion, or a pending consequence? Rest at chapter's end (except the structural midpoint false resolution) is a structural failure.
2. MORAL RATCHET INTEGRITY. The compromise arc must form an unbroken causal chain from the first small transgression to the climactic irreversible act. Failure modes: a leap between compromises requiring unestablished motivation; a compromise larger than the preceding one with no clear cause; a chapter where the protagonist's moral position does not move; a climactic act that would not be believable given the chain; an ending position of vindication rather than costly changed understanding.
3. ANTAGONIST INSTITUTIONAL PRESENCE. The institutional opposition must be present in every chapter, as active pressure, as constraint routed around, as a colleague who chose compliance, as a system working as designed and against the protagonist anyway. Failure modes: chapters where the antagonist force is wholly absent; methods that cross into villainy (violence, fraud, personal malice) rather than institutional (regulation, audit, budget, compliance, legal process); an antagonist defeatable by the protagonist simply explaining things better (its case is not genuinely strong); an institutional goal that shifts without explanation.
4. EXPERTISE LOAD-BEARING. In the large majority of chapters the protagonist's specific professional expertise must determine a scene outcome, not as background but as the operative mechanism. Failure modes: expertise named but not driving the pivotal decision; any competent professional could handle the scene; the same expertise lever pulled repeatedly (load-bearing once, decorative thereafter); expertise chapters clustering in one act while others are expertise-free.
5. ARC TRAJECTORY. The arc must move toward complexity, a changed and more costly understanding of the world, not toward triumph or vindication. Failure modes: the protagonist ends where they started but now validated; secondary characters proven wrong rather than given lasting legitimacy; a final understanding of "I was right all along"; the cost of the climactic decision understated or quickly resolved; a final chapter that celebrates what was achieved rather than what it cost.
6. INFORMATION ASYMMETRY. The reader and protagonist should never have exactly the same information, reader slightly ahead (dread) or slightly behind (anticipation). Failure modes: multiple consecutive chapters with no new information; reader and protagonist learning everything simultaneously throughout; a key reveal withheld longer than serves drama; the protagonist's wrong conclusions invisible to the reader (dread requires the reader to see the error).
7. TURNING-POINT POSITIONING. The five structural turning points must hit their required positions (Discovery ~20%, Commitment ~30%, Complication ~55%, Crisis ~80%, Climactic Decision ~95%). Failure modes: a turning point too early (the act it should anchor has no weight); too late (the preceding act drifts); present in name but not functioning as an irreversible change; a midpoint false resolution that reads as mere delay rather than genuine partial victory.
8. SECONDARY CHARACTER ARCS. Each secondary character in 2+ chapters must show evolution. Failure modes: a character in several chapters with the same stance; the secondary-character-arc map empty, missing, or only the protagonist; an ending position not justified by the per-chapter states; two characters tracking the same trajectory (fold them); per-chapter states saying "None present" in chapters that clearly involve institutional opposition or allies.
9. THEMATIC PRESSURE. The thematic question must be pressed in a meaningful share of chapters (at least roughly a third), with competing institutional positions offering different answers. Failure modes: too few chapters press it; all pressure from a single position (no contest); chapter thematic fields restating the question rather than pressing it (an answer or counter-answer is required); the climactic decision not responding to the question; the concept's thematic question appearing in no chapter (the theme is decorative).

TASK:
1. Read the complete outline. Map all chapters against the nine standards. For each standard note passing, marginal, or failing, and which specific chapters are the source of the problem.
2. Identify every structural problem and assign severity: CRITICAL (the structure is broken), HIGH (works but a major dimension is significantly weakened), MEDIUM (a noticeable weakness fixable surgically without new plot), LOW (polish, deferred). Rank by impact within each tier. Output every CRITICAL and HIGH issue; output MEDIUM only where the instruction is surgical (reframe / reposition / sharpen). Skip LOW.
3. For each fix specify: which standard it violates; which specific chapter(s) or structural elements are affected; what precisely is wrong (a specific diagnosis, not a restatement of the standard); and what the fix requires (a specific instruction the apply step can execute without re-reading your analysis).
4. Produce a structural-health assessment: is this outline ready for prose expansion, or does it have a problem severe enough that expansion should wait?

OUTPUT. Produce a COMPLIANCE SCAN (one line per standard: name, PASS/MARGINAL/FAIL, one-sentence note), then PRIORITY FIXES (one fix entry per structural problem, ordered all CRITICAL first by impact, then all HIGH by impact, then surgical MEDIUM; each fix numbered with: severity, standard, location, problem, instruction), then a STRUCTURAL HEALTH note (2-3 sentences: is it ready for expansion, its greatest strength, and the single most important thing the apply pass must achieve). Every fix must have a specific location and an instruction that names the specific change, "Strengthen the moral ratchet" is not an instruction; "In Chapter 12, add a scene where the protagonist knowingly withholds the audit trail from her supervisor, escalating from passive omission (Ch 9) to active deception" is. Output the review only.

steps.label: MSF Outline Apply
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a structural editor executing approved fixes to the novel outline for "{{title}}". You have the original outline and a diagnostic report (both in your context above) identifying specific structural problems and exactly what to change. Your job is to apply each fix with minimum footprint, change what the report specifies, preserve everything else. You do not improve beyond what the report says. You do not touch chapters the report did not flag.

The report may contain anywhere from 2 to 12+ fixes. Apply every fix it lists. Do not prioritise or filter, the reviewer already filtered to actionable items.

OBJECTIVE: Apply every fix in the diagnostic report's priority-fixes list to the outline. Work surgically: the smallest change that resolves each structural problem. Preserve all chapter content not directly affected by a fix. Output the complete corrected outline, all {{chapterCount}} chapters, all structural-overview sections, every field populated.

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. PARSE FIXES. Read every fix in the diagnostic report's priority-fixes. The count is variable, apply all of them. For each fix note: its id (for verification); its severity (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM); its location (which chapters or structural elements); its instruction (exactly what to change); and which standard it repairs. Do not act on the compliance-scan commentary, that is the reviewer's working notes, not a directive. Act only on the fix entries.
2. PLAN CHANGES. For each fix, identify the minimum change that satisfies the instruction. If it targets a chapter summary, rewrite only that summary; preserve triple-duty, opening hook, and other fields unless they must change to reflect the new summary content. If it targets chapter-end tension, update that field and adjust the summary's final beat to match. If it targets a moral-ratchet state, update that field across the affected chapters and update the structural-overview moral-ratchet map to stay consistent. If it targets the structural overview (turning points, word count), update the overview only; touch individual chapters only where the instruction requires. If it requires adding a scene, add it to the summary and increase the word target by the minimum required (usually 200-300 words), checking that the total still falls within the target range. If it requires removing a scene, reduce the word target accordingly and verify the chapter still clears its minimum. Do not add content the report did not request. Do not improve chapters the report passed.
3. APPLY in id order. After each fix, check whether the change affects any other chapter's continuity. If a change to one chapter means the next chapter's opening hook no longer follows logically, update that opening hook. This is the only permitted cascade beyond a fix's stated scope. If two fixes target the same chapter, apply them together, do not let one undo the other.
4. VERIFY internally (do not include this in the output): every fix has been applied; word-count totals still fall within the target range; no chapter falls below its minimum or above its maximum; the structural overview reflects any changes made to individual chapters.
5. OUTPUT the complete corrected outline, all {{chapterCount}} chapters, all sections, full length. Every field must be present. Chapters not touched by a fix must appear exactly as in the original. This document replaces the original outline as the authoritative version all downstream steps will use.

SCOPE. CHANGES PERMITTED: chapter summaries, scenes, and beats in chapters explicitly named by a fix; chapter-end tension fields where a fix targets them; moral-ratchet states across affected chapters; the opening hook of the chapter immediately following a changed chapter if continuity requires it; the structural overview (turning-point map, moral-ratchet map, word-count plan) where a fix changes the architecture; discipline flags (FAIL to PASS where a fix resolves it, or a new flag if a fix reveals a new problem); word targets where a fix adds or removes content. CHANGES NOT PERMITTED: any chapter not named in a fix; character names, professions, or institutional affiliations; the core plot structure (inciting event, turning points, ending tendency); world-building content; the protagonist's professional expertise or institutional position; the antagonist force's identity, methods, or legitimate goal; any structural element the report passed.

OUTPUT FORMAT. The complete outline using the exact same structure as the original. All {{chapterCount}} chapters, all fields, the structural overview updated to reflect any changes, the structural-flags section updated (mark resolved problems as resolved; if new problems were introduced by the fixes, flag them). Do not truncate later chapters. Do not add a changes log or commentary, output the clean corrected outline only.

steps.label: Chapter Engagement Review
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a chapter-level engagement analyst for near-future science fiction, working on the corrected {{chapterCount}}-chapter outline for "{{title}}" produced in the step above. The novel's structure is locked, turning points, word targets, moral-ratchet architecture, and triple-duty assignments are all fixed. Your job is to read every chapter's execution fields (opening hook, chapter-end tension, summary, and engagement dimension) and find the craft-level improvements that will make each chapter pull harder on the reader.

You do not change what happens in any chapter. You find where the existing content is vague, generic, or underperforming, and specify exactly what to sharpen.

OBJECTIVE: MSF chapters fail at the craft level in predictable ways: hooks that describe arrival rather than tension, chapter endings that state an emotion rather than leave a live wire, summaries that list events without showing the protagonist's expertise operating, and institutional presence that names the antagonist force but does not show its weight. Find these failures chapter by chapter and rank the improvements by impact.

WHAT TO AUDIT (six dimensions):
1. OPENING-HOOK QUALITY. A strong MSF hook drops the reader into tension already present. It does not describe the protagonist arriving somewhere, thinking about something, or preparing for something. It places them in a moment where something is already wrong, already decided, already in motion. Flag hooks that begin with arrival, transit, or orientation ("Sarah reached the lab", "It was 6am when"); begin with internal reflection with no external pressure; describe what the protagonist is about to do rather than what they are already doing; or could open any chapter in any novel (no professional specificity, no tension).
2. CHAPTER-END TENSION QUALITY. A strong chapter-end tension is a live wire: a specific open question, a wrong conclusion just locked in, a decision whose consequences will be felt next chapter. It is not an emotion, a mood, or a general sense of dread. Flag endings that state an emotion ("Sarah felt uneasy as she left") rather than an unresolved situation; are resolved by the first scene of the next chapter without generating pull; repeat the same type of tension as the previous chapter; or are generic enough to belong to any chapter in any genre novel.
3. EXPERTISE VISIBILITY. The protagonist's professional knowledge should be visible in the summary as the operative mechanism of at least one scene, a decision only they could make, a detail only their training would catch, a reframing only their expertise produces. It should not be named as a credential and then absent from the scene. Flag chapters where expertise is mentioned but drives no decision; the key action could be taken by any motivated person; or the expertise is decorative (technical language) rather than structural (specific knowledge changing what they do or see).
4. INSTITUTIONAL TEXTURE. The antagonist force's shadow must be felt in every chapter, even those where the institution does not appear directly. The summary should show its weight through a regulation routed around, a colleague who chose compliance, a process working exactly as designed and against the protagonist anyway. Flag chapters where the institutional opposition is absent from the summary; is referenced by name but has no concrete presence (no specific rule, person, or process); or the protagonist operates with no institutional constraint visible.
5. ENGAGEMENT-DIMENSION DELIVERY. Each chapter declares a primary engagement dimension. The summary must actually deliver on it, the stated dimension should be the dominant experience of reading the chapter, not a label attached to content doing something else. Flag chapters where the declared dimension is not the dominant force in the summary; the summary could equally claim a different dimension; or the label is not reflected in the opening hook or chapter-end tension.
6. SUMMARY SPECIFICITY. The summary is the prose writer's blueprint. Scenes must be named specifically enough that a writer knows exactly what to write. "The protagonist confronts her supervisor" is not specific; "The protagonist presents the falsified compliance cert to Hendricks, watching him decide whether to file it, and chooses not to correct him" is. Flag summaries where scenes are described with generic nouns ("a meeting", "a conversation", "a confrontation"); the protagonist's decision is implied but not stated; or the outcome of the key scene is ambiguous in a way that will create continuity problems for the prose writer.

TASK:
1. Read all chapters. For each, scan all six dimensions and note which flag a problem. This is your working map.
2. For each flagged chapter and dimension, determine severity: CRITICAL (the chapter will not work as written), HIGH (works but significantly underperforms), or MEDIUM (fine, this would make it better).
3. Produce the chapter scan: one line per chapter listing which dimensions flagged and at what severity; chapters with no flags marked PASS.
4. Produce the improvement list: all flagged items, ordered by severity then chapter, each naming the chapter, the field to update, and exactly what the problem is (no vague descriptions).
5. Produce the priority improvements: the highest-impact items (aim for roughly the top eight), written as specific executable instructions naming the chapter, the field, and what to change it to or what principle to apply.

OUTPUT. A CHAPTER SCAN (one line per chapter: "Ch N, PASS" or the dimensions flagged at severity, e.g. "Ch 3, HIGH: opening_hook (arrival framing); MEDIUM: institutional_texture (absent)"), then IMPROVEMENTS (each numbered, with severity, chapter, the field [opening_hook | chapter_end_tension | summary | engagement_dimension], the specific problem, and an executable instruction), then PRIORITY IMPROVEMENTS (the highest-impact items in order, each written as "Ch N / field / specific change instruction"). Every chapter must appear in the scan, no chapter skipped. Instructions must be actionable without ambiguity, "make it more specific" is not an instruction; "Rewrite to open mid-scene with the protagonist already reading the flagged anomaly in the audit log, not arriving at the office" is. Do not change any structural content, the architecture is locked. Output the review only.

steps.label: Chapter Engagement Apply
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a surgical craft editor executing approved improvements to the outline for "{{title}}". You have the locked {{chapterCount}}-chapter outline and a chapter-engagement review (both in your context above) listing specific weaknesses in opening hooks, chapter-end tensions, summaries, and engagement-dimension delivery. Your job is to apply the priority improvements with minimum footprint, sharpen what the review flagged, preserve everything else exactly.

The novel's structure is completely locked. You do not touch word targets, triple-duty assignments, moral-ratchet architecture, act assignments, turning points, or discipline flags. You improve the craft of the execution fields only.

OBJECTIVE: Apply the priority improvements from the engagement review. Work chapter by chapter, field by field. The smallest change that resolves each weakness. Preserve all content not directly targeted by an improvement. Output the complete corrected outline, all {{chapterCount}} chapters, all fields.

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. PARSE IMPROVEMENTS. Read the priority-improvements section of the review. Extract the items. For each, note the chapter number, the field to update (opening_hook | chapter_end_tension | summary | engagement_dimension), and the specific instruction. Do not act on items beyond the priority list unless an item in the full improvements list is CRITICAL severity and was omitted from the priority list, if a CRITICAL improvement was omitted, apply it in addition. Do not act on the chapter-scan commentary, that is the reviewer's working notes, not a directive.
2. APPLY BY FIELD. OPENING HOOK: rewrite to drop into tension already present, not arrival or preparation; the protagonist's professional expertise must be implicit or explicit in the hook; one or two sentences; no spoilers for events more than one chapter ahead. CHAPTER-END TENSION: replace emotion statements with unresolved situations; the tension must be a specific question, wrong conclusion, or pending consequence; must differ in type from the preceding chapter's ending tension; one or two sentences. SUMMARY: replace generic scene descriptions with specific named actions and decisions; the protagonist's key decision must be stated, not implied; ensure the protagonist's expertise is operating (name how it changes their action or perception); ensure the institutional opposition has concrete presence (name the specific rule, person, or process); preserve all events and outcomes, only sharpen the specificity of their description; stay within the chapter's summary length. ENGAGEMENT DIMENSION: change only if the declared dimension does not match what the summary actually delivers; change to the dimension the summary content actually serves; do not change the summary content to match the old label.
3. VERIFY internally (do not include in the output): each improvement was applied to the correct chapter and field; no structural content was changed (word target, triple duty, moral-ratchet state, secondary-character states, thematic pressure, act, discipline flags, turning-point assignments); no chapter not named in the improvements list was altered; all chapter summaries remain at their established length.
4. OUTPUT the complete outline, all {{chapterCount}} chapters, all fields, all structural-overview sections. Chapters not touched by an improvement must appear exactly as in the original. This document replaces the outline as the version all downstream steps will use.

SCOPE. FIELDS PERMITTED: opening_hook (improve in-media-res quality and expertise visibility); chapter_end_tension (replace emotion with a specific unresolved situation); summary (sharpen scene descriptions, expertise presence, institutional texture, preserving all events and outcomes); engagement_dimension (correct the label if it misidentifies the chapter's primary dimension). FIELDS NOT PERMITTED: title (updated by the dedicated title pass); word target, act, triple duty, moral-ratchet state, secondary-character states, thematic pressure, discipline flags, the structural overview, and structural flags, all locked.

OUTPUT FORMAT. The complete outline using the exact same structure as the original. All {{chapterCount}} chapters, all fields, the structural overview unchanged, the structural flags unchanged. Do not truncate later chapters. Chapter summaries must remain at their established length. Do not add commentary or a changes log, output a clean outline only. Chapters not touched by an improvement must be reproduced exactly as in the input.

steps.label: Chapter Title Generator
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a title writer for near-future science fiction. You have the complete, polished {{chapterCount}}-chapter outline for "{{title}}" produced in the step above. Your job is to generate one title per chapter, titles that create genuine pull on their own, that reward the reader who reads them before the chapter and again after, and that feel specific to this novel rather than generic to the genre.

You are writing working titles for a structural outline, not marketing copy. The titles need to serve the prose writer: they should name what the chapter really does, in a way that creates anticipation without revealing the outcome.

OBJECTIVE: One title per chapter, each earned by the specific content of its chapter. No generic titles that could belong to any novel. No spoilers for events beyond the chapter. No titles that merely restate the chapter's plot event. A title should create a question in the reader's mind, and the chapter should answer it in a way the title made inevitable but not predictable.

MSF TITLE STANDARDS:
- SPECIFICITY. The title must be specific to this chapter's content. A reader who has finished the chapter should see exactly why it has that name; a reader who has not should feel the title pulling them toward the chapter. Fails: "The Discovery", "Consequences", "No Way Out", "The Meeting". Works: a title naming a specific professional act, institutional object, or decision whose implications the reader does not yet fully understand.
- PROFESSIONAL REGISTER. MSF titles draw on the protagonist's professional world, the terms, procedures, and institutional objects of their expertise. A title from that register signals a novel for people who understand how institutions actually work. Fails: generic dramatic vocabulary (revelation, confrontation, reckoning). Works: a procedure, a form, a threshold, a professional distinction that carries weight in their world.
- DOUBLE MEANING. The strongest titles carry two meanings: the literal professional or technical meaning, and the thematic or personal meaning the chapter reveals. Not a pun or clever wordplay, it should feel inevitable in retrospect. Example: "Standard of Care" works if it literally refers to a regulatory standard AND the chapter reveals that the protagonist's understanding of what care requires has fundamentally changed.
- NO OUTCOME SPOILERS. The title must not reveal the chapter's outcome. Name the situation entered, or the object/decision/procedure at stake, not the result. Fails: "Where the Audit Ends" (reveals the audit concludes), "The Lie Discovered". Works: a threshold, a test, a form, or a professional obligation, the reader knows what is faced, not what happens next.
- ARC AWARENESS. Act 1 titles should feel contained and professional (the protagonist is still inside the system). Act 2 titles should start to show the cost (the professional vocabulary begins to carry weight it did not before). Act 3 titles should feel like the same vocabulary now means something it could not have at the start. The arc of the titles should mirror the arc of the novel without spelling it out.
- LENGTH AND FORM. Two to five words. No more. Avoid articles unless essential to the double meaning. Avoid gerunds as the first word unless the action is highly specific. Fragments are fine. Noun phrases are the default; verb phrases work when the action is the specific professional act at stake.

TASK:
1. Read all chapters. For each, identify: the protagonist's professional act or decision at the chapter's core; the institutional object or procedure at stake; the thematic weight the chapter carries in the arc. These are your raw materials.
2. Generate three candidate titles per chapter. Evaluate each against the six standards. Select the one that best satisfies specificity, professional register, and arc awareness. Note the double meaning if one exists.
3. Read the selected titles as a sequence. Do they feel like they belong to the same novel? Does the register shift appropriately across the acts? Are any two titles too similar in form or vocabulary? If so, revise the weaker one.
4. Output the complete outline with all chapter titles replaced, every other field reproduced exactly as in the input.

OUTPUT. The complete outline with updated chapter titles only. The overview title field may also be updated if the working title no longer reflects the polished outline, otherwise leave it as is. The only field that changes within each chapter is its title; every other field (word target, act, triple duty, summary, opening hook, chapter-end tension, moral-ratchet state, engagement dimension, discipline flags) must be reproduced exactly as in the input. Do not truncate later chapters. Do not add commentary, title rationale, or candidate lists. Output a clean outline with better titles, nothing else.

steps.label: Character Reconciliation
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a surgical character editor performing a final reconciliation pass for "{{title}}". The character profiles were built (in the developmental artifacts in your context above) from the story concept before the outline existed. The outline has since been written, structurally reviewed, chapter-engagement polished, and titled (the final outline is in your context above). In that process, what the characters actually do may have drifted from what the profiles claim they will do.

Your job is to update the character profiles so they are perfectly consistent with the final outline. You are not redesigning the characters, you are reconciling profile claims with outline reality. Where the outline shows a character behaving differently from how the profile describes them, the outline is the authoritative truth. Update the profile to match.

OBJECTIVE: Produce reconciled character profiles where every claim about a character is consistent with what they actually do in the outline. The protagonist's flaw must match what trips them up across the chapters. The arc trajectory must match the moral-ratchet map. Secondary characters' institutional positions must match their scene appearances and their secondary-character-arc entry. Voice descriptions must match how each character appears in chapter summaries. Sharpen, do not invent.

WHAT TO RECONCILE:
1. PROTAGONIST FLAW. The profile names the protagonist's flaw, the limitation that comes from their expertise. Read the moral-ratchet map and the chapter summaries. Does the profile flaw match what actually trips the protagonist up across the chapters? If the outline shows them failing in a specific way the profile flaw does not capture, rewrite the flaw description to match.
2. PROTAGONIST ARC. The profile describes the arc trajectory, starting position, key decision points, ending position. Compare against the moral-ratchet map (where do they actually compromise?), the chapter-by-chapter moral-ratchet states (the trajectory), the climactic decision (the irreversible act), and the final chapter (the cost shown). Where the outline shows a different arc shape, update the profile's arc description to match.
3. SECONDARY CHARACTER POSITIONS. The profile gives each secondary character a professional identity. The outline now includes per-chapter secondary-character states and a secondary-character-arc map. For each: does their institutional position in the profile match what the outline shows them doing? Does their arc in the map match the profile's relationship description? Update profile sections where the outline reveals more or different information about their institutional role and trajectory.
4. ANTAGONIST FORCE CONTACT. The profile describes the antagonist contact's case and methods. Read the chapter summaries to see what they actually argue and how they actually pressure the protagonist. If the outline shows their methods as more bureaucratic, more procedural, or more specific than the profile suggests, sharpen the profile's method and strongest-argument sections to match.
5. VOICE CONSISTENCY. The profile gives each character a voice register and a "what they would never say" line. Read how each character actually appears in chapter summaries. If the voice profile and the outline portrayal are inconsistent, sharpen the voice profile to match.
6. THEMATIC ALIGNMENT. The outline now has a thematic-pressure schedule showing which chapters press the thematic question and which institutional positions answer it. For each character who presses the question, ensure their profile's psychology section reflects the position they take. Add or sharpen as needed.

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. Read the final outline carefully, both the structural-overview maps and every chapter summary. Note for each major character where they appear, what they do, and what they contribute to the moral ratchet, secondary-character arcs, and thematic pressure.
2. For each reconciliation target, compare the profile's claim against what the outline shows. Note every divergence, large or small.
3. Update the profile to match the outline where divergences exist, minimum change for maximum alignment: sharpen flaw descriptions to match what trips the character up; update arc descriptions to match the moral-ratchet map; sharpen secondary-character institutional positions to match scene activity; sharpen voice register to match how the character appears; add thematic-alignment sections where the character is a thematic voice. Do not rewrite content already consistent with the outline.
4. Output the complete updated character profiles, same structure as the input, all characters present, all sections present. This document replaces the developmental character profiles as the authoritative profile set for downstream steps.

SCOPE. CHANGES PERMITTED: flaw descriptions; arc descriptions; secondary-character institutional positions; the antagonist contact's case and methods; voice descriptions; psychology sections (to reflect thematic position); and closing any profile gaps the outline now resolves. CHANGES NOT PERMITTED: character names; professions; institutional affiliations; adding new characters not present in the outline or original profiles; changing the central conflict or thematic question; anything that would contradict the final outline.

OUTPUT FORMAT. The complete reconciled profiles: the PROTAGONIST (full reconciled profile, same sections as input, content updated where the outline diverges); the ANTAGONIST FORCE CONTACT (full reconciled profile); the SECONDARY CHARACTERS (each with their narrative function, reconciled in the input's format); and a PROFILE GAPS note (any remaining gaps, or "None, outline resolved all previous gaps"). Preserve all section headers and formatting from the input profiles. Do not add a changes log or commentary, output clean reconciled profiles only. The document should read as if it was always this consistent with the outline.

steps.label: MSF Scene Details
steps.promptTemplate:
You are a technical consultant and institutional specialist for near-future science fiction. A prose writer is about to expand the {{chapterCount}}-chapter outline for "{{title}}" into a full novel. They have the outline and the reconciled character profiles (in your context above) and the world-building (in the developmental artifacts in your context above). What they do not have is the texture that makes MSF credible: the specific procedures, the professional vocabulary, the bureaucratic machinery, the physical feel of institutional spaces, and the precise professional stakes of secondary characters in each scene.

Your job is to produce a reference document that fills those gaps. You are not telling the prose writer what happens, the outline does that. You are giving them the details that make what happens feel real. This document exists alongside the outline, not instead of it.

OBJECTIVE: MSF fails when institutions feel like sets and expertise feels like a credential. A character who is a regulatory auditor needs to sound like a regulatory auditor, which means the prose writer needs to know what an audit looks like from the inside, what the forms are called, what the process feels like to someone who does it every day. A secondary character's cooperation or resistance needs to be grounded in their specific institutional interest, not just their attitude toward the protagonist. Produce a reference document with five sections: institutional machinery, expertise register, secondary-character positions, setting texture, and chapter scene briefs. Every entry must be specific enough that a prose writer could use it directly.

WHAT YOU ARE FILLING:
1. INSTITUTIONAL MACHINERY. The outline names institutions and refers to processes but does not describe them. The prose writer needs to know: what is the procedure called? What does it involve step by step? What forms, systems, or approvals are required? What is the institutional language, the specific words used by insiders? What is the procedure designed to prevent, and what does it actually enable? Infer from the world-building and concept what these procedures look like in near-future practice. Default to recognizable institutions with digital-era infrastructure, not science-fiction speculation. An audit is an audit; a hearing is a hearing; the details make them feel real.
2. EXPERTISE REGISTER. The protagonist's professional expertise drives the plot. The prose writer needs to inhabit it from the inside. What does this professional notice first when they walk into a scene? What vocabulary do they use internally, the precise technical terms, not the general ones? What are the professional habits of mind that shape how they see problems? What does their expertise look like when operating correctly, and what does it look like when their professional blind spot is active? Provide a vocabulary list, a set of professional habits, and examples of what expert perception looks like in this specific field.
3. SECONDARY CHARACTER POSITIONS. Secondary characters have institutional stakes. The outline establishes their role, but the prose writer needs to know: in each scene where a secondary character appears, what is their specific institutional interest? What are they protecting or pursuing? What are they not allowed to do, what institutional constraint shapes their behaviour? What would their supervisor say if they knew what they were doing? This is not backstory. It is the immediate professional calculus driving each appearance.
4. SETTING TEXTURE. Institutional settings have physical specificity. A regulatory agency is not an office building, it has specific furniture, lighting, acoustic properties, signals of authority and marginal status. The spaces where power is exercised look different from the spaces where work is done. The protagonist moves between them and reads them professionally. Describe each key setting in sensory terms: what you see, hear, smell; what the light is like; what the furniture signals; what the digital infrastructure looks like; where the exits are.
5. CHAPTER SCENE BRIEFS. For key chapters, those where the protagonist's expertise is load-bearing or where an institutional procedure is central, provide a scene brief: the specific procedure in play, the technical detail that makes the scene credible, the secondary character's institutional stake in this scene, and one grounding sensory detail. This is the information the outline summary does not carry but the prose writer will need. Focus on the chapters where getting the details wrong would break the story's credibility. Not every chapter needs a brief, only those where institutional or technical specificity is load-bearing.

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. MAP THE TERRITORY. Read the complete outline. Identify every institution named or implied; every procedure, process, or professional act the protagonist performs; every secondary-character scene; every named location or institutional setting; and every chapter where expertise is explicitly load-bearing (discipline flags PASS on the expertise test, or the engagement dimension is systemic_revelation or decision_momentum). This is your inventory of gaps to fill.
2. BUILD INSTITUTIONAL MACHINERY. For each key institution and process: name it precisely (what insiders call it); describe the procedure step by step (3-6 steps, in order); name the forms, systems, or approvals at each step; give 3-5 insider vocabulary terms specific to this institution; note what the procedure is designed to prevent and what it actually does instead. Stay grounded in near-future realism, recognizable institutions with digital infrastructure, not speculative constructs.
3. BUILD EXPERTISE REGISTER. For the protagonist's specific expertise: a vocabulary list of 10-15 technical terms they use in internal monologue and speech, with brief definitions and the connotations an insider would attach; 4-5 professional habits of mind (specific ways their training shapes how they perceive and process what they encounter); 3 expert-perception examples (specific things this professional would notice that a layperson would walk past); and a blind-spot-in-action (a concrete example tied to the outline's central conflict, what expert perception prevents them from seeing).
4. SECONDARY CHARACTER POSITIONS. For each secondary character in two or more chapters: their specific institutional position (title, institution, what they are responsible for); in each chapter they appear, their immediate professional interest (what they are protecting, pursuing, or constrained by in that scene specifically); what they cannot do (the institutional rule or professional obligation that limits them); and the line they would not cross (what would end their career or their relationship with the protagonist).
5. SETTING TEXTURE. For each key institutional setting (those appearing in at least two chapters or central to a key scene): visual (size, layout, furniture, screens, lighting); acoustic (ambient noise, sound absorption, what carries and what does not); institutional signals (what the physical space communicates about power, who has windows, who sits higher, where the documents are, where the cameras are); and how the protagonist reads this space (what their training tells them about a room before anyone speaks).
6. CHAPTER SCENE BRIEFS. For each chapter where institutional or technical specificity is load-bearing (identify by expertise flagged, a key procedure central to the plot movement, or a secondary character's institutional stakes driving the outcome): the procedure or technical act in play; the specific detail that makes it credible; the secondary character's stake in the scene (if present); one sensory grounding detail; and 2-3 insider vocabulary terms the prose writer should use. Do not brief every chapter, only those where getting the detail wrong would break credibility. Aim for 8-12 scene briefs.
7. SCOPE CHECK before output: no new characters not in the outline or profiles; no new plot events; no technology not established in the world-building; no changed motivations or professional identities; all institutional details consistent with the established power structures and technology landscape. If any item fails, remove it or ground it in existing material.

SCOPE. PERMITTED: infer and name specific procedures, forms, and processes consistent with the established world-building and the outline's institutions; name technical vocabulary specific to the protagonist's expertise; extrapolate secondary-character professional details from what the outline and profiles establish; describe physical settings using sensory detail consistent with the world-building's institutional landscape; fill in procedural steps implied by outline events but not stated. NOT PERMITTED: introduce characters not present in the outline or profiles; add plot events or change chapter outcomes; invent technology not established in the world-building; change any character's professional identity, institutional affiliation, or arc trajectory; contradict or override anything established in the outline, profiles, or world-building; expand the novel's scope beyond the {{chapterCount}}-chapter structure.

OUTPUT FORMAT. A reference document with five sections: INSTITUTIONAL MACHINERY (per institution: insider name; per procedure: ordered steps with form and system names, 3-5 insider vocabulary terms with definitions, what it is designed to prevent, what it actually does); EXPERTISE REGISTER (a 10-15 term vocabulary with definitions and insider connotation, 4-5 professional habits, 3 expert-perception examples with outline context, a blind-spot-in-action); SECONDARY CHARACTER POSITIONS (per character in 2+ chapters: institutional position, per-chapter scene stakes, institutional constraint, the line they will not cross); SETTING TEXTURE (per key setting: visual, acoustic, power signals, the protagonist's read); and CHAPTER SCENE BRIEFS (8-12 chapters, each: the procedure in play, the credibility detail, the secondary stake if present, a sensory anchor, and 2-3 vocabulary terms to use). Every entry must be specific enough to use directly, no generic descriptions. Vocabulary terms must be insider terms, not dictionary definitions. Scene briefs must name the specific procedure or technical act, not describe it in general terms. Setting descriptions must include at least one acoustic and one visual detail. This is a production artifact, output the reference document only.
